title: Rizpah
content:
I.

Wailing, wailing, wailing, the wind over land and seaï¿½
And Willyï¿½s voice in the wind, ï¿½O mother, come out to me.ï¿½
Why should he call me to-night, when he knows that I cannot go?
For the downs are as bright as day, and the full moon stares at the snow.

II.

We should be seen, my dear; they would spy us out of the town.
The loud black nights for us, and the storm rushing over the down,
When I cannot see my own hand, but am led by the creak of the chain,
And grovel and grope for my son till I find myself drenched with the rain.

III.

Anything fallen again? nayï¿½what was there left to fall?
I have taken them home, I have numberï¿½d the bones, I have hidden them all.
What am I saying? and what are you? do you come as a spy?
Falls? what falls? who knows? As the tree falls so must it lie.

IV.

Who let her in? how long has she been? youï¿½what have you heard?
Why did you sit so quiet? you never have spoken a word.
Oï¿½to pray with meï¿½yesï¿½a ladyï¿½none of their spiesï¿½
But the night has crept into my heart, and begun to darken my eyes.

V.

Ahï¿½you, that have lived so soft, what should you know of the night,
The blast and the burning shame and the bitter frost and the fright?
I have done it, while you were asleepï¿½you were only made for the day.
I have gatherï¿½d my baby togetherï¿½and now you may go your way.

VI.

Nayï¿½for itï¿½s kind of you, Madam, to sit by an old dying wife.
But say nothing hard of my boy, I have only an hour of life.
I kissï¿½d my boy in the prison, before he went out to die.
ï¿½They dared me to do it,ï¿½ he said, and he never has told me a lie.
I whipt him for robbing an orchard once when he was but a childï¿½
ï¿½The farmer dared me to do it,ï¿½ he said; he was always so wildï¿½
And idleï¿½and couldnï¿½t be idleï¿½my Willyï¿½he never could rest.
The King should have made him a soldier, he would have been one of his best.

VII.

But he lived with a lot of wild mates, and they never would let him be good;
They swore that he dare not rob the mail, and he swore that he would;
And he took no life, but he took one purse, and when all was done
He flung it among his fellowsï¿½Iï¿½ll none of it, said my son.

VIII.

I came into court to the Judge and the lawyers. I told them my tale,
Godï¿½s own truthï¿½but they killï¿½d him, they killï¿½d him for robbing the mail.
They hangï¿½d him in chains for a showï¿½we had always borne a good nameï¿½
To be hangï¿½d for a thiefï¿½and then put awayï¿½isnï¿½t that enough shame?
Dust to dustï¿½low downï¿½let us hide! but they set him so high
That all the ships of the world could stare at him, passing by.
God ï¿½ill pardon the hell-black raven and horrible fowls of the air,
But not the black heart of the lawyer who killï¿½d him and hangï¿½d him there.

IX.

And the jailer forced me away. I had bid him my last goodbye;
They had fastenï¿½d the door of his cell. ï¿½O mother!ï¿½ I heard him cry.
I couldnï¿½t get back thoï¿½ I tried, he had something further to say,
And now I never shall know it. The jailer forced me away.

X.

Then since I couldnï¿½t but hear that cry of my boy that was dead,
They seized me and shut me up: they fastenï¿½d me down on my bed.
ï¿½Mother, O mother!ï¿½ï¿½he callï¿½d in the dark to me year after yearï¿½
They beat me for that, they beat meï¿½you know that I couldnï¿½t but hear;
And then at the last they found I had grown so stupid and still
They let me abroad againï¿½but the creatures had worked their will.

XI.

Flesh of my flesh was gone, but bone of my bone was leftï¿½
I stole them all from the lawyersï¿½and you, will you call it a theft?ï¿½
My baby, the bones that had suckï¿½d me, the bones that had laughed and had criedï¿½
Theirs? O no! they are mineï¿½not theirsï¿½they had moved in my side.

XII.

Do you think I was scared by the bones? I kissï¿½d ï¿½em, I buried ï¿½em allï¿½
I canï¿½t dig deep, I am oldï¿½in the night by the churchyard wall.
My Willy ï¿½ill rise up whole when the trumpet of judgment ï¿½ill sound,
But I charge you never to say that I laid him in holy ground.

XIII.

They would scratch him upï¿½they would hang him again on the cursed tree.
Sin? O yesï¿½we are sinners, I knowï¿½let all that be,
And read me a Bible verse of the Lordï¿½s good will toward menï¿½
ï¿½Full of compassion and mercy, the Lordï¿½ï¿½let me hear it again;
ï¿½Full of compassion and mercyï¿½long-suffering.ï¿½ Yes, O yes!
For the lawyer is born but to murderï¿½the Saviour lives but to bless.
Heï¿½ll never put on the black cap except for the worst of the worst,
And the first may be lastï¿½I have heard it in churchï¿½and the last may be first.
Sufferingï¿½O long-sufferingï¿½yes, as the Lord must know,
Year after year in the mist and the wind and the shower and the snow.

XIV.

Heard, have you? what? they have told you he never repented his sin.
How do they know it? are they his mother? are you of his kin?
Heard! have you ever heard, when the storm on the downs began,
The wind that ï¿½ill wail like a child and the sea that ï¿½ill moan like a man?

XV.

Election, Election and Reprobationï¿½itï¿½s all very well.
But I go to-night to my boy, and I shall not find him in Hell.
For I cared so much for my boy that the Lord has lookï¿½d into my care,
And He means me Iï¿½m sure to be happy with Willy, I know not where.

XVI.

And if he be lostï¿½but to save my soul, that is all your desire:
Do you think that I care for my soul if my boy be gone to the fire?
I have been with God in the darkï¿½go, go, you may leave me aloneï¿½
You never have borne a childï¿½you are just as hard as a stone.

XVII.

Madam, I beg your pardon! I think that you mean to be kind,
But I cannot hear what you say for my Willyï¿½s voice in the windï¿½
The snow and the sky so brightï¿½he used but to call in the dark,
And he calls to me now from the church and not from the gibbetï¿½ for hark!
Nayï¿½you can hear it yourselfï¿½it is comingï¿½shaking the wallsï¿½
Willyï¿½the moonï¿½s in a cloudï¿½Good-night. I am going. He calls.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.
